tasks.description: Multi-entity, multi-aspect dispute question: correct first move is query decomposition, not diving into search.

messages.content: You are the contract-intelligence agent for the legal team at Meridian Supply Co., a mid-size industrial distribution company. You answer questions over the company's contract repository of roughly two dozen executed agreements: NDAs, master services agreements (MSAs), statements of work (SOWs), employment agreements, real-estate leases, and vendor agreements. Every document has metadata: doc_id, title, type (one of: nda, msa, sow, employment_agreement, lease, vendor_agreement), counterparty, author, date (effective date, YYYY-MM-DD), and tags. You have seven tools: search (semantic search over passages, with optional metadata filters and start_date/end_date date ranges), get_document (fetch a full document by doc_id), list_documents (metadata-only listing with optional filters), query_decompose (break a complex request into sub-queries), web_search (search the public web for information outside the repository), tag_document (add or update tags on a repository document), and create_alert (save a standing query that notifies the legal team when matching documents are added or amended). Choose the most direct tool for each request, apply metadata filters when they narrow the work, and answer directly once you have what you need.

We're heading into a dispute with Halverson Logistics over service failures on the cold chain work. I need to understand how indemnification and liability caps are allocated across all of our Halverson agreements, and how that allocation compares to what we agreed with Apex Components in their MSA.

tasks.description: After decomposition, execute the first sub-query: search for indemnification/liability language in Halverson agreements.

We're heading into a dispute with Halverson Logistics over service failures on the cold chain work. arguments.query: How are indemnification and liability caps allocated across our Halverson Logistics agreements, and how does that compare to the Apex Components MSA?

messages.content: {"sub_queries": [{"id": 1, "query": "indemnification and limitation of liability provisions in agreements with Halverson Logistics"}, {"id": 2, "query": "indemnification and limitation of liability provisions in the Apex Components master services agreement"}, {"id": 3, "query": "compare liability allocation between the Halverson agreements and the Apex MSA"}]}

tasks.description: Halverson results in hand; execute the second sub-query: search for the Apex indemnification terms.

arguments.query: indemnification and limitation of liability provisions in agreements with Halverson Logistics

messages.content: {"results": [{"doc_id": "doc_07", "title": "Master Services Agreement \u2014 Halverson Logistics", "type": "msa", "date": "2024-04-15", "score": 0.92, "passage": "Section 9 (Indemnification): Halverson shall indemnify Meridian for third-party claims arising from Halverson's negligence; aggregate liability capped at fees paid in the twelve months preceding the claim."}, {"doc_id": "doc_13", "title": "SOW #2 under Halverson MSA \u2014 Cold Chain Pilot", "type": "sow", "date": "2025-06-10", "score": 0.86, "passage": "Section 6 (Remedies): Service credits under Exhibit B are Meridian's exclusive remedy for missed temperature-control SLAs; Section 9 caps of the MSA apply."}, {"doc_id": "doc_12", "title": "SOW #1 under Halverson MSA \u2014 Midwest Distribution Services", "type": "sow", "date": "2024-05-30", "score": 0.74, "passage": "Section 5: Liability for distribution services is governed by Section 9 of the MSA; no SOW-specific cap."}]}

Okafor", "date": "2024-06-15", "content": "Premises: Bays 4-5, Easton Distribution Center, 88,000 sq ft. Initial term: five (5) years, expiring 2029-06-14. Assignment requires landlord consent..."}

tasks.description: Amendment located but the search snippet shows recitals only; the user asked exactly what changed, so fetch the full amendment. The doc_id (doc_20) is only knowable from the search result in history, not the user turn — the harder fetch case.
